docs.title: MLRI Platform Wiki
docs.text: Open this page every time you sit down to work. It tells you where things stand.

docs.title: Current Phase
docs.text:
Phase 1: Connect to Brightspace · Months 1–2

Working toward: OAuth API connection, pulling core learner data, displaying it in the custom frontend layer, and getting SSO working so learners have a single login.

See Phase 1 details and task checklist →

docs.title: Open Questions — Needs Decisions
docs.text:
These are blocking or important enough to track. Update this list as decisions get made (and record them in the Decision Log).

  • Versioning threshold — When do we archive a course and create a new offering vs. updating in place? Define the line between "minor" and "major."
  • Learner update notices — If enrolled learners need to know about a significant content change, how do we reach them? (Email may not be reliable enough for legal updates.)
  • Sections vs. new Offerings — For different cohorts or scheduled runs, do we use Brightspace Sections inside one Offering, or create separate Offerings?
  • Discover page vs. custom catalog — Are we using Brightspace's built-in Discover page, our own custom frontend catalog, or both?

Full decision log →

docs.title: Quick Links
docs.text:
I want to... Go to... See the full project roadmap Phase Overview Look up what a Brightspace term means Glossary Check our naming rules Naming Conventions Understand how our org is structured Org Hierarchy Record a decision or check past decisions Decision Log Do a common admin task Quick Tasks Understand our course update policy Versioning & Archiving

Last updated: 2026-05-13

docs.title: Decision Log
docs.text:
Every significant decision about how we build and run the MLRI Brightspace system lives here — what was decided, when, and why. The "why" is the most important part. It prevents future-you from second-guessing choices that were made for good reasons.

Open decisions are at the top. Resolved ones are below.

docs.title: Versioning Threshold
docs.text:
Question: When do we archive a course and create a new offering vs. updating the existing one in place? We need a clear line between "minor" and "major."

Why it matters: Without a defined threshold, this becomes a judgment call every time — and over time, inconsistent decisions make the catalog messy. A clear rule also helps us communicate to learners when they need to retake something.

Options being considered: - Archive only when the law changes or >30–40% of content is replaced - Archive any time a new assessment is added - Archive any time completion requirements change

Status: Open · Added 2026-05-13 See: Versioning & Archiving policy

docs.title: Learner Update Notices
docs.text:
Question: If enrolled learners need to know about a significant content change, how do we reach them reliably? Brightspace email has deliverability limitations.

Why it matters: Legal professionals need accurate information. If a law changes and we update a course, we need to be confident the right people actually see the notice — not just that we sent an email that may have gone to spam.

Options being considered: - Brightspace email only (available now, but unreliable for critical notices) - Custom frontend dashboard notification (reliable, but not built until Phase 3) - Direct outreach by the training team for critical updates

Status: Open · Added 2026-05-13 See: Learner Update Notices policy

docs.title: Sections vs. New Offerings for Cohorts
docs.text:
Question: When we have different cohorts, organizations, or scheduled runs of the same course — do we use Brightspace Sections inside one Offering, or create a separate Offering for each?

Why it matters: This affects enrollment management, completion reporting, and how much administrative overhead is created for every new cohort.

Options being considered: - Sections inside one Offering (less clutter, but may complicate reporting) - Separate Offerings per cohort (cleaner reporting, but more objects to manage)

Status: Open · Added 2026-05-13

docs.title: Discover Page vs. Custom Catalog
docs.text:
Question: Are we using Brightspace's built-in Discover page, our own custom frontend catalog, or both for learners to find courses?

Why it matters: This affects Phase 1 scope and the long-term UX of the platform. Running both requires keeping them in sync.

Status: Open · Added 2026-05-13

docs.title: Use Training Area as a new hierarchy layer
docs.text:
Decision: Add a Training Area layer between Massachusetts and Program in the Brightspace hierarchy.

Why: Without it, the catalog would be a flat list of Programs with no navigational grouping. As the catalog grows, learners and admins would have no way to quickly filter or browse by content type.

What we decided: Start with two Training Areas: Legal Skills and Substantive Law.

Resolved: 2026-05-13 · See Training Areas

docs.title: Use Program → Course Template → Course Offering structure
docs.text:
Decision: Organize content as: Program → Course Template → Course Offering (with optional Section).

Why: This structure supports evergreen courses by giving each "course family" a container (Template) that holds all versions. It also separates the learner-facing name (Offering) from the administrative container (Template), keeping the catalog clean while preserving version history.

Resolved: 2026-05-13 · See Org Hierarchy

docs.title: Naming convention for templates and archived offerings
docs.text:
Decision: Templates use [Name] Template. Archived offerings use ARCHIVED: [Name] V#. Current offerings use a clean name with no prefix or version number visible to learners.

Why: Makes administrative views scannable. The ARCHIVED prefix is immediately visible in dropdown lists and search. The clean name for current offerings keeps the learner experience tidy.

Resolved: 2026-05-13 · See Naming Conventions

To add a new decision: copy the Open template above, fill in the question and context, and add today's date.

docs.title: MLRI Org Hierarchy in Brightspace
docs.text: This is how MLRI's content is organized inside Brightspace. Every course, program, and training area fits into this structure. Understanding the hierarchy is the foundation for every other decision — naming, versioning, user access, and reporting.

docs.title: The Hierarchy
docs.text:
MLRI
└── Massachusetts
    ├── Legal Skills  (Training Area)
    │   ├── Intro to Legal Skills  (Program)
    │   │   └── Legal Writing Template  (Course Template)
    │   │       ├── ARCHIVED: Legal Writing V1  (Course Offering)
    │   │       └── Legal Writing V2  (Course Offering — current)
    │   └── Client Communication  (Program)
    │       └── ...
    └── Substantive Law  (Training Area)
        ├── Housing Law  (Program)
        │   └── Eviction Defense Basics Template  (Course Template)
        │       └── Eviction Defense Basics  (Course Offering — current)
        └── Benefits Law  (Program)
            └── ...

docs.title: MLRI
docs.text: The top-level organization. Everything lives under MLRI in Brightspace.

docs.title: Massachusetts
docs.text: The current state/jurisdiction level. This layer exists to leave room for future expansion — other states, regions, partner organizations, or business units could be added here later without restructuring everything else.

docs.title: Training Area
docs.text:
Broad content categories that help learners navigate and discover courses. Currently:

  • Legal Skills — Skills applicable across all practice areas
  • Substantive Law — Area-specific legal content

See Training Areas for full definitions.

docs.title: Program
docs.text: Groups related learning experiences together. A Program is a named curriculum or topic cluster, like Housing Law or Intro to Legal Skills. Learners may take multiple Course Offerings within a Program over time.

docs.title: Course Template
docs.text: A container that holds all versions of a course. For example, Legal Writing Template holds Legal Writing V1, V2, and any future revisions. Templates are not learner-facing — learners enroll in Course Offerings, not templates. Templates exist to keep version history organized.

docs.title: Course Offering
docs.text: The actual learner-facing course. This is where content, assessments, discussions, completion tracking, and activities live. When you enroll a learner, you enroll them in an Offering.

docs.title: Section (optional)
docs.text: A subdivision of a Course Offering. May be useful for different cohorts, organizations, or scheduled runs of the same course. Not required for every course — especially self-paced evergreen content.

docs.title: Why This Structure
docs.text:
The key design decision here is supporting evergreen content — courses that stay live and get updated over time rather than being run once and archived. The hierarchy gives us:

  • A clean place for multiple versions of a course to coexist (Course Template)
  • A named home for related courses to be discovered together (Program)
  • Room to grow into other jurisdictions or partner orgs without restructuring (Massachusetts layer)
  • Separation between what learners see (Offerings) and how we manage versions (Templates)

docs.title: Naming Conventions
docs.text: Consistent naming keeps the Brightspace backend clean as the catalog grows. These rules apply to everything we create in Brightspace.

docs.title: Course Templates
docs.text:
Use the course name followed by "Template".

Legal Writing Template
Eviction Defense Basics Template
Client Intake Template

Templates are not learner-facing, so the name is purely administrative. The word "Template" makes it immediately obvious this is a container, not an enrollable course.

docs.title: Current Course Offerings
docs.text:
Use a clean, readable name — no version number in the title unless needed for clarity. The "current" offering is just the clean name.

Legal Writing
Eviction Defense Basics
Client Intake Fundamentals

Why no version number on the current offering? Learners see this name. "Legal Writing" reads better than "Legal Writing V2." The version is tracked inside the template container.

docs.title: Archived Course Offerings
docs.text:
Prefix with "ARCHIVED:" and include the version number.

ARCHIVED: Legal Writing V1
ARCHIVED: Eviction Defense Basics V1

Archived offerings should also be hidden from learners so they don't accidentally enroll. The ARCHIVED prefix makes them easy to spot in admin views.

docs.title: Programs
docs.text:
Plain descriptive name — no special prefix. Should be clear to a learner browsing a catalog.

Intro to Legal Skills
Housing Law
Benefits Law
Client Communication

docs.title: Training Areas
docs.text:
Broad, stable category names. We currently have two:

Legal Skills
Substantive Law

See Training Areas for what belongs in each.

docs.title: Quick Reference
docs.text: Item Format Example Course Template [Name] Template Legal Writing Template Current Offering Clean name, no prefix Legal Writing Archived Offering ARCHIVED: [Name] V# ARCHIVED: Legal Writing V1 Program Plain descriptive name Housing Law Training Area Broad category Legal Skills

docs.title: When to Increment the Version Number
docs.text: The version number (V1, V2) tracks major revisions — not minor edits. See Versioning & Archiving for the full policy on when to create a new version vs. editing in place.

docs.title: Training Areas
docs.text:
Training Areas are the top-level content categories in MLRI's Brightspace catalog. They sit below the Massachusetts level and above Programs. Every Program belongs to a Training Area.

Training Areas serve two purposes:

  1. Learner navigation — They help attorneys find relevant courses when browsing the catalog or using the Discover page.
  2. Administrative organization — They group related Programs for reporting and management.

docs.title: Legal Skills
docs.text:
Courses that teach skills applicable across all practice areas — skills every attorney needs regardless of what kind of law they practice.

What belongs here: - Legal writing - Client communication and intake - Legal research methods - Courtroom and advocacy skills - Document drafting - Professional responsibility and ethics - Case management workflows

What does NOT belong here: - Anything specific to a substantive area of law (housing, benefits, immigration, etc.) — those go in Substantive Law

docs.title: Substantive Law
docs.text:
Courses about specific areas of law — the rules, statutes, regulations, and procedures for a particular legal topic.

What belongs here: - Housing law (eviction defense, landlord-tenant) - Benefits law (SNAP, MassHealth, SSI, etc.) - Immigration - Family law - Consumer protection - Any other area-specific legal content

What does NOT belong here: - General legal skills that would apply in any practice area — those go in Legal Skills

docs.title: The "When in doubt" rule
docs.text:
Ask: Is this course useful to an attorney who doesn't practice in this specific area?

  • Yes → Legal Skills
  • No, it's specific to a practice area → Substantive Law

docs.title: Future Training Areas
docs.text:
The Training Area layer was designed to grow. Future additions might include:

  • Organizational — internal MLRI policies, onboarding, HR-adjacent training
  • Technology — tools, systems, software training for staff
  • Partner Organizations — training shared with outside partners or coalitions

Adding a new Training Area is a significant decision. It changes catalog navigation and affects how learners find content. Document the decision in the Decision Log before creating a new one in Brightspace.

docs.title: Project Roadmap — Three Phases
docs.text: MLRI's Brightspace + custom platform build is organized into three phases over approximately 5–6 months. The goal is to move from a basic Brightspace connection to a full custom data layer and reporting suite that supports MLRI's legal education mission.

docs.title: Phase Timeline
docs.text: Phase Name Months Status 1 Connect to Brightspace 1–2 🟡 In progress 2 Own Data Layer 2–4 ⬜ Not started 3 Reporting & Evaluation 4–6 ⬜ Not started

docs.title: Phase 1 · Connect to Brightspace (Months 1–2)
docs.text:
Get a working, authenticated connection to the Brightspace API and surface core learner data in our custom frontend.

Key deliverables:

  • Brightspace API OAuth 2.0 connection with token management
  • Pull: enrollments, completions, grades, time-in-course
  • Display Brightspace data in the custom frontend discovery layer
  • SSO so learners have a single login — no double sign-in

Phase 1 details →

docs.title: Phase 2 · Own Data Layer (Months 2–4)
docs.text:
Build our own database (Supabase/Postgres) to capture behavioral data that Brightspace cannot track on its own.

Key deliverables:

  • Supabase backend for custom events and user data
  • Search tracking: what learners search for, what they don't find
  • Engagement events: time on page, click paths, drop-off points
  • Learner feedback: ratings, content usefulness flags

Phase 2 details →

docs.title: Phase 3 · Reporting & Evaluation (Months 4–6)
docs.text:
Surface data to learners, managers, and the MLRI team through dashboards and evaluation tools.

Key deliverables:

  • Learner dashboard: personal progress, completions, recommendations
  • Manager view: team completion rates, gaps, activity
  • Program evaluation: cohort analysis, equity gaps, completion predictors
  • Population insights: who's completing, who isn't, and why — by learner segment

Phase 3 details →

docs.title: System Stack — How the Layers Connect
docs.text:
Learner / Manager / Admin (Browser)
            ↕
┌─────────────────────────┐    ↔    ┌─────────────────────────┐
│   Your Frontend Layer   │         │        Supabase          │
│  Discovery · Progress   │         │  Custom events · Users   │
└─────────────────────────┘         └─────────────────────────┘
            ↕
   Brightspace REST API (OAuth 2.0)
            ↕
   Brightspace (D2L)
   Course content · Completions · Grades · Enrollments

docs.title: From Brightspace API
docs.text:
• Enrollment status
  • Course completions
  • Quiz / assessment scores
  • Time in course
  • Last accessed date
  • Grade data

docs.title: From Our Own Layer (Supabase)
docs.text:
• Search queries and results
  • Click paths and navigation
  • Time on page (frontend layer)
  • Content ratings and flags
  • Learning path choices
  • Drop-off points

docs.title: What We Surface (Phase 3 Outputs)
docs.text:
• Learner progress dashboards
  • Manager team views
  • Program completion rates
  • Cohort equity analysis
  • Content effectiveness signals
  • Catalog gap analysis

docs.title: Phase 1 · Connect to Brightspace
docs.text:
Timeline: Months 1–2 · Status: 🟡 In progress

The goal of Phase 1 is a working, authenticated connection to the Brightspace API and core learner data surfaced in our custom frontend. Everything in Phase 2 and 3 depends on this foundation being solid.

docs.title: Brightspace API Auth
docs.text:
• Set up OAuth 2.0 connection to Brightspace
  • Implement token management (storage, refresh)
  • Test connection in Brightspace sandbox environment
  • Document the auth flow for future reference

docs.title: Pull Core Data
docs.text:
• Enrollments — who is in which course
  • Completions — who has finished what
  • Grades — quiz and assessment scores
  • Time-in-course — how long learners spend
  • Last accessed — when someone was last active

docs.title: Display in Frontend
docs.text:
• Wire Brightspace data into the custom discovery layer
  • Learner can see their enrolled courses
  • Learner can see their completion status
  • Basic progress display working end-to-end

docs.title: SSO / Auth
docs.text:
• Single login — learner authenticates once, gets into both the frontend and Brightspace
  • No double sign-in required
  • Identity flow tested with a real account

docs.title: Notes & Blockers
docs.text:
Use this section for notes as you work through Phase 1. Add dates when things happen.

  • (Add notes here)

docs.title: Resources
docs.text:
• Brightspace API documentation — D2L's official API docs
  • Phase Overview — Full roadmap context

docs.title: Phase 2 · Own Data Layer
docs.text:
Timeline: Months 2–4 · Status: ⬜ Not started

Brightspace tracks what happens inside its own courses — but it can't capture how learners interact with our custom frontend: what they search for, where they get lost, what content they rate highly. Phase 2 builds a Supabase (Postgres) database to capture exactly that.

docs.title: Supabase Backend
docs.text:
• Set up Supabase project and database schema
  • Define tables for: users, events, search logs, feedback
  • Connect frontend to Supabase
  • Set up row-level security so only appropriate data is accessible

docs.title: Search Tracking
docs.text:
• Log what learners search for in the catalog
  • Log what results they get (and click vs. skip)
  • Log "zero results" searches — content gaps
  • Build a way to review search logs periodically

docs.title: Engagement Events
docs.text:
• Track time on page (frontend layer, not Brightspace)
  • Log click paths — how learners navigate the catalog
  • Log path navigation patterns
  • Track drop-off points — where learners leave without completing

docs.title: Learner Feedback
docs.text:
• Build a lightweight rating mechanism (e.g., 👍/👎 or stars)
  • Allow learners to flag content (outdated, unclear, etc.)
  • Store usefulness signals by content item
  • Decide: is feedback anonymous or attributed?

docs.title: Notes & Blockers
docs.text:
Use this section for notes as you work through Phase 2. Add dates when things happen.

  • (Add notes here)

docs.title: Open Questions for Phase 2
docs.text:
• What's the privacy policy for behavioral tracking? Do learners need to be informed?
  • Is feedback anonymous or tied to learner accounts?
  • What's the data retention policy for event logs?

docs.title: Resources
docs.text:
• Phase Overview — Full roadmap context
  • Supabase documentation

docs.title: Phase 3 · Reporting & Evaluation
docs.text:
Timeline: Months 4–6 · Status: ⬜ Not started

Phase 3 is where the data from Phases 1 and 2 gets turned into something useful for learners, managers, and MLRI's program team. The equity and population insights work here is tied directly to MLRI's mission — understanding who is completing training and who isn't.

docs.title: Learner Dashboard
docs.text:
• Learner can see their enrolled courses and completion status
  • Learner can see a history of what they've completed
  • Recommendations surfaced based on role or past activity
  • Mobile-friendly layout

docs.title: Manager View
docs.text:
• Manager can see completion rates for their team
  • Manager can identify gaps — who hasn't completed required training
  • Activity summary (last accessed, time spent)
  • Export or share options for reporting up

docs.title: Program Evaluation
docs.text:
• Cohort analysis — how did a specific group do in a specific course?
  • Equity gap analysis — are certain groups completing at lower rates?
  • Completion predictors — what factors correlate with finishing?
  • Content effectiveness — which modules have high drop-off?

docs.title: Population Insights
docs.text:
• Who is completing training — by learner segment
  • Who isn't completing — and why (if data supports it)
  • Catalog gap analysis — what topics are being searched for but not found?
  • Reporting format for stakeholders (funders, leadership)

docs.title: Notes & Blockers
docs.text:
Use this section for notes as you work through Phase 3. Add dates when things happen.

  • (Add notes here)

docs.title: Open Questions for Phase 3
docs.text:
• What "learner segments" are meaningful for equity analysis at MLRI? (e.g., by office, practice area, years of experience?)
  • Who has access to the Manager View? How is "manager" defined?
  • What does the stakeholder report need to include? (For funders, board, etc.)
  • How do we handle update notices on the learner dashboard? (See Learner Update Notices policy)

docs.title: Resources
docs.text: • Phase Overview — Full roadmap context

docs.title: Learner Update Notices Policy
docs.text:
When MLRI makes a major update to a course, some enrolled learners may need to know — especially if the change involves a law or regulation that affects their practice. This policy covers how we decide who needs to know, what we tell them, and how we reach them.

This policy is not fully decided yet

The notification mechanism is still an open question. Brightspace email may not be reliable enough for important legal updates. The custom frontend dashboard is the likely long-term solution, but it's not built yet (Phase 3). Document the final decision in the Decision Log.

docs.title: Step 1 — Decide Who Needs to Know
docs.text:
Not every update requires notifying enrolled learners. Use this framework:

Scenario Action Minor update (typo, link fix, wording) No notification needed Major update — law or regulation changed Notify affected learners Major update — instructional overhaul Notify if completion of new version should be required New offering added, old archived Notify only if learners need to retake

docs.title: Step 2 — Decide What Action to Ask For
docs.text:
When notifying learners, be clear about what (if anything) they need to do:

  • No action required — "We updated this course. Your completion record is unchanged."
  • Recommended review — "The law changed in this area. We recommend reviewing the updated content."
  • Required retake — "This course has been substantially revised. Please complete the new version by [date]."

Avoid vague notices. If the change is important enough to notify about, be specific.

docs.title: Option A: Brightspace Email (current only option)
docs.text:
Brightspace can send email notifications to enrolled learners. Limitations:

  • Email deliverability is not guaranteed
  • Many attorneys use shared or monitored inboxes
  • No confirmation that the learner saw the message
  • Not recommended for critical legal updates on its own

Use for: Low-stakes notices, reminders, optional recommendations.

docs.title: Option B: Custom Frontend Dashboard (future — Phase 3)
docs.text:
The long-term solution is a notification or banner in the learner's custom dashboard that appears when they log in. This is much more visible than email and confirms the learner actually saw it.

Use for: All important legal update notices, required retakes.

Status: Not built yet. This is a Phase 3 deliverable. Until Phase 3 is complete, email is the only available channel.

docs.title: Option C: Direct Outreach
docs.text: For critical updates affecting a small, known group — a practice area team, a cohort — direct outreach by the training team (email, Slack, a meeting) may be the most reliable option.

docs.title: Documenting Notifications
docs.text:
When you send a learner notification, record it in the Decision Log:

  • What course was updated
  • Why it was a major update
  • Who was notified and how
  • What action was requested
  • Date sent

docs.title: User Management
docs.text:
This page covers how users are added, managed, and removed in Brightspace — and what roles mean at MLRI.

Fill this in as you learn it

Some details here (specific role names, your org's exact onboarding process) will depend on how MLRI's Brightspace instance is configured. Update this page as you establish those processes.

docs.title: Roles in Brightspace
docs.text:
Brightspace uses roles to control what a user can see and do. The standard roles relevant to MLRI are:

Role What they can do Learner Enroll in and complete courses. No admin access. Instructor / Course Author Create and edit course content. May not be needed for all staff. Course Administrator Manage enrollments, sections, and course settings for assigned courses. Org Administrator Full access to the Brightspace org — user management, course creation, system settings.

Who at MLRI has Org Administrator access? Document this here once established.

docs.title: Onboarding a New User
docs.text:
1. Confirm their role — What do they need to do in Brightspace? (Just take courses? Create content? Manage enrollments?)
  2. Create their account — [Add the steps for your org's process here — self-registration, bulk import, or manual creation]
  3. Assign the correct role — Match the role to what they need (see table above)
  4. Enroll them in relevant courses — If they're a Learner, enroll them in their required or recommended courses
  5. Send a welcome message — [Note if your org has a standard onboarding email or welcome course]

docs.title: Offboarding a User
docs.text:
When someone leaves MLRI or no longer needs Brightspace access:

  1. Preserve their completion records — Do NOT delete the account if they have completion history. Completion data is tied to the account.
  2. Deactivate, don't delete — Deactivate the account so they can no longer log in, but their records remain
  3. Remove from active course enrollments — Unenroll from courses they won't complete
  4. Document the date — Note when access was removed and why

Why not delete? Deleting a user account removes their completion history, which may be needed for reporting, compliance, or if the attorney later rejoins.

docs.title: Bulk Enrollment
docs.text:
For onboarding a cohort or a new practice area team, Brightspace supports bulk enrollment via CSV import.

  • Document the process here once your org establishes it

docs.title: Open Questions
docs.text:
• Does MLRI use self-registration, or are all accounts created by an admin?
  • Is there a standard onboarding course new users should complete first?
  • Who is responsible for offboarding when someone leaves?

docs.title: Versioning & Archiving Policy
docs.text:
Because MLRI courses are evergreen — meaning learners may revisit them over time and content may change as laws or best practices evolve — we need a clear policy for when to update a course in place vs. when to create a new version.

This policy is not fully decided yet

The threshold between "minor" and "major" updates is still an open question. The framework below reflects our working understanding. When this is finalized, update this page and mark the decision in the Decision Log.

docs.title: The Core Distinction
docs.text: Update Type What to do Minor Edit the existing live course Major Create a new Course Offering, archive the old one

docs.title: Minor Updates — Edit in Place
docs.text:
A minor update is a change that does not affect the learning outcome or require learners to retake the course.

Examples of minor updates: - Fixing typos or grammatical errors - Updating broken links - Refreshing examples without changing the substance - Clarifying confusing wording - Adding a note or callout for context - Updating formatting or visuals

What to do: Make the edit directly in the live Course Offering. No version increment, no archive needed.

docs.title: Major Updates — Create a New Version
docs.text:
A major update is a change that meaningfully affects what the course teaches — either because the law changed, the instructional approach changed, or the content was substantially replaced.

Examples of major updates: - A law, regulation, or court decision changes what attorneys need to know - Substantial content replacement (more than ~30–40% of a module rewritten) - New required material added that all learners should complete - New assessments or completion requirements - A fundamentally different teaching approach for the same topic

What to do: 1. Create a new Course Offering under the same Course Template 2. Name it with a clean title (current version gets the clean name, old gets ARCHIVED: prefix) 3. Hide the old offering from learners so they can't accidentally enroll 4. Decide whether existing enrolled learners need to take the new version (see Learner Update Notices)

docs.title: Archiving Checklist
docs.text:
When creating a new version:

  • New offering created under the correct Course Template
  • Old offering renamed: ARCHIVED: [Name] V#
  • Old offering hidden from learner view
  • New offering is the clean current name
  • Decision documented in the Decision Log (what changed and why)
  • Learner notification decision made (see Learner Update Notices)

docs.title: Why We Don't Delete Old Versions
docs.text:
Archived offerings are kept (hidden, not deleted) for several reasons:

  • Learners who completed the old version have a completion record tied to that offering
  • Compliance or grant reporting may require evidence of what content existed at a point in time
  • It lets us compare old and new content if questions arise

docs.title: Glossary
docs.text: Key terms used in Brightspace and in this wiki. When someone on the team uses one of these terms, this is what they mean at MLRI.

docs.title: Course Offering
docs.text:
The actual learner-facing course — where content, assessments, discussions, completion tracking, and learning activities live. When you enroll a learner, you enroll them in an Offering. A Course Template may contain multiple Offerings (different versions over time).

At MLRI: This is what attorneys see and interact with. Name it cleanly — no internal jargon or version numbers visible to learners.

docs.title: Course Template
docs.text:
An organizational container that holds all versions of a course. Templates are not learner-facing — learners don't enroll in templates, they enroll in Offerings inside a template. Templates exist to keep version history organized without cluttering the main catalog.

At MLRI: Named [Course Name] Template (e.g., Legal Writing Template). Contains the current Offering and any ARCHIVED versions.

docs.title: Section
docs.text:
An optional subdivision of a Course Offering. Can be used for different cohorts, scheduled runs, or organizations taking the same course. Not required — especially for self-paced evergreen content.

At MLRI: Whether to use Sections vs. separate Offerings for cohorts is still an open decision.

docs.title: Program
docs.text:
A named grouping of related Course Templates. For example, Housing Law is a Program that contains multiple course templates related to housing and eviction law.

At MLRI: Programs live inside Training Areas. A learner might be "in the Housing Law program," meaning they're working through courses in that cluster.

docs.title: Training Area
docs.text:
A broad content category that groups related Programs. Currently: Legal Skills and Substantive Law. Helps learners navigate the catalog.

At MLRI: The layer below Massachusetts in the hierarchy, above Programs. See Training Areas for full definitions.

docs.title: Org (Organization)
docs.text: The top-level entity in Brightspace. Everything lives under the Org. At MLRI, the Org is MLRI itself.

docs.title: Discover
docs.text: Brightspace's built-in course catalog/discovery page. Learners can browse and self-enroll in courses through Discover. Whether MLRI uses the built-in Discover page or a custom frontend catalog is an open decision.

docs.title: Completion
docs.text: A record in Brightspace that a learner has finished a course (or met whatever completion criteria were set). Completions are tied to the Course Offering and the learner account — this is why we deactivate accounts rather than deleting them.

docs.title: Enrollment
docs.text: The connection between a learner account and a Course Offering. A learner can be enrolled manually by an admin, bulk-imported via CSV, or self-enrolled through Discover.

docs.title: Evergreen Course
docs.text: A course designed to stay live and be updated over time, rather than being run once and closed. Most MLRI courses are evergreen — attorneys may revisit them as the law changes or as they need a refresher.

docs.title: Major Update
docs.text: A change to a course that meaningfully affects what it teaches — typically triggered by a law change, substantial content replacement, or new completion requirements. Requires creating a new Course Offering and archiving the old one. See Versioning & Archiving.

docs.title: Minor Update
docs.text: A change to a course that does not affect the learning outcome — typo fixes, broken links, refreshed examples. Can be made directly in the live Offering without creating a new version.

docs.title: Archived Offering
docs.text: A Course Offering that has been superseded by a newer version. Named ARCHIVED: [Name] V# and hidden from learners. Preserved so completion records remain intact.

docs.title: Quick Tasks
docs.text: Fast answers for common Brightspace admin tasks. No background reading required.

docs.title: Create a new Course Offering
docs.text:
1. Go to Admin Tools → Course Management
  2. Click Create a Course
  3. Set the Template to the correct Course Template (e.g., Legal Writing Template)
  4. Name it following the naming conventions
  5. Set the correct Program and Training Area
  6. Set availability dates if applicable
  7. Save

docs.title: Archive an old Course Offering
docs.text:
1. Go to the Course Offering you want to archive
  2. Rename it: ARCHIVED: [Name] V#
  3. Go to Course Settings → set Active to Inactive (or set an end date in the past)
  4. Hide it from the Discover page / catalog
  5. Record the archival in the Decision Log

docs.title: Copy content from one Offering to another
docs.text:
Used when creating a new version from an existing course:

  1. Open the new (destination) Course Offering
  2. Go to Course Tools → Import / Export / Copy Components
  3. Choose Copy Components from Another Org Unit
  4. Select the source course
  5. Choose which components to copy (modules, assessments, etc.)
  6. Review and complete the copy

docs.title: Add a single user
docs.text:
1. Go to Admin Tools → Users
  2. Search for the person — they may already have an account
  3. If not, click Create a User
  4. Fill in name, email, username
  5. Assign the correct Role (Learner, Instructor, etc.)
  6. Save

docs.title: Enroll a user in a course
docs.text:
1. Go to the Course Offering
  2. Go to Classlist
  3. Click Add Participants → Add Existing Users
  4. Search for the user
  5. Assign their role in the course (usually Learner)
  6. Enroll

docs.title: Deactivate a user (when someone leaves)
docs.text:
1. Go to Admin Tools → Users
  2. Find the user
  3. Open their profile
  4. Set Is Active to No (do NOT delete — this preserves completion records)
  5. Note the date and reason

docs.title: Make a minor edit to a live course
docs.text:
1. Go to the Course Offering
  2. Open the module or topic to edit
  3. Make your change
  4. Save — the change is live immediately

No versioning or notification needed for minor edits (typos, links, wording). See Versioning & Archiving for what counts as minor vs. major.

docs.title: Check if a learner completed a course
docs.text:
1. Go to the Course Offering
  2. Go to Grades or Progress
  3. Search for the learner by name
  4. View their completion status and date

docs.title: Find a course in the org
docs.text: Admin Tools → Course Management → search by name or course code

docs.title: Find all courses in a Training Area or Program
docs.text: Admin Tools → Course Management → filter by Org Unit Path

docs.title: See who is enrolled in a course
docs.text:
Open the Course Offering → Classlist

Add steps here as you learn them. Date your additions so you know when things were written.
